findings.summary: The book directly and repeatedly documents the lack of demonstrated efficacy in people over 75 (citing Pfizer's December 10, 2020 FDA presentation by William Gruber and the OPECST report), making this one of its central claims.

references.quote: The reported efficacy is 100%, but the associated range is -13.1% to 100%. As a biostatistician, this indicates that there is no statistical evidence of vaccine efficacy in individuals over 75 years old. There were too few of them included at the time of the interim analysis to establish such evidence.
references.relevance: Cotton's core statement that there is no statistical evidence of efficacy in over-75s, sourced to William Gruber's Dec 10, 2020 FDA presentation (footnote 30 links to fda.gov/media/144325/download). The accompanying table shows the >=75 years group with VE 100.0 and 95%CI (-13,1, 100,0).

references.quote: On page 17, they once again confirmed one of my observations with the statement, “The efficacy could not be established for individuals over 75 years of age due to small sample sizes.” Consequently, this vulnerable population, often under treatment, was vaccinated without statistically demonstrated efficacy.
references.relevance: Documents that the official December 15, 2020 OPECST/Assemblee Nationale report ('The Covid-19 Vaccine Strategy', footnote 192) itself acknowledged efficacy could not be established for over-75s -- 'written in the reports' as Cotton says in the interview.

references.quote: However, the results presented cannot confirm the vaccine’s efficacy against severe forms of COVID19, asymptomatic forms, or in individuals over 75 years old.
references.relevance: Quotes the February 2021 VIDAL website (footnote 32) confirming the data could not establish efficacy in over-75s or against severe forms, reinforcing that the limitation was on the public record.

findings.summary: The book does not address the leaked Pfizer-South Africa supply contract or any Health Justice Initiative disclosure declaring Phase 2-3 trials 'completed'; it discusses U.S. Department of Defense Pfizer contracts and EU contract transparency, but not a South African supply contract.

The book quotes the Moderna SEC filing (December 2019 10-K) verbatim as its smoking-gun evidence that mRNA was classified by the FDA as a gene therapy product, exactly the document the follow-up seeks.

references.quote: The information given by Moderna to the US SECURITIES AND EXCHANGE COMMISSION as early as December 2019 should have alerted us.
references.relevance: Introduces the Moderna SEC filing; footnote 295 links directly to sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1682852/.../moderna10-k12312019.htm -- the exact 10-K the todo asks to locate.

references.quote: One can read: "Currently, mRNA is considered a gene therapy product by the FDA. In addition, because no product in which mRNA is the primary active ingredient has been approved, the regulatory pathway for approval is uncertain.
references.relevance: Verbatim quote from the Moderna 10-K classifying mRNA as a gene therapy product -- the reclassification 'smoking gun' Cotton references in the interview.

references.quote: The FDA has known all along that mRNA is a gene therapy, COVID vaccines were not classified as such to allow pharmaceutical companies to have an accelerated development for their vaccines, regulation far less restrictive than GT regulation which would have required more studies and more time !
references.relevance: Cotton's interpretation of the SEC filing as evidence of deliberate non-classification to enable accelerated development -- the gene-therapy-vs-vaccine argument.

findings.summary: The book describes and reproduces the FDA VRBPAC October 22, 2020 'Preliminary list of VAERS AEs of special interest' slide (Guillain-Barre, encephalitis, myocarditis, stroke, etc.) and even cites the FDA YouTube video and slide-deck download links.

references.quote: During the FDA’s Vaccine and Related Biological Products Advisory Committee (VRBPAC) public meeting, which was broadcast live on October 22, 2020, this man, who occupies a position of prime importance—and a military one at that—featured a page on adverse events of special interest (AESI).
references.relevance: Identifies the exact VRBPAC Oct 22, 2020 meeting and AESI slide; footnote 91 links to the FDA YouTube channel video (youtube.com/watch?v=1XTiL9rUpkg).

references.quote: On October 22, 2020, before the first results of the clinical trials, these gentlemen, who cannot be accused of being "conspiracy theorists" or "anti-vaxxers", had identified the following pathologies as adverse events to be monitored from the VAERS or VSD database:
references.relevance: Introduces the full reproduced AESI list (Guillain-Barre Syndrome, encephalitis, seizures, stroke, myocarditis/pericarditis, thrombocytopenia, venous thromboembolism, etc.), proving harms were anticipated before rollout. Footnotes 92-94 link to the FDA slide-deck downloads (fda.gov/media/...).

references.quote: • Guillain-Barré Syndrome,
references.relevance: Specific item from the reproduced AESI list named in the todo, alongside encephalitis and myocarditis/pericarditis which also appear in the same list.

findings.summary: The book documents the Process 1 vs Process 2 manufacturing change and states that the published protocol limited Process 2 validation to 250 participants (compared against 250 Process 1 vaccinees), the exact 'product administered is not the product tested' argument the todo concerns.

references.quote: This change in manufacturing process was already mentioned into the published protocol. 250 participants were to receive vaccines from process 2. Immunogenicity (antibody assay) and safety of this process 2 were to be compared with a sample of 250 randomly selected vaccinated with process 1.
references.relevance: Directly states the C4591001 protocol limited Process 2 validation to 250 participants -- the specific protocol provision the todo asks to locate. Footnote 296 links to the NEJM protocol PDF.

references.quote: The public was led to believe they were receiving a vaccine with the 95% efficacy announced in December 2020, which was actually based on participants in the trial who had received process 1 or clinical trial material.
references.relevance: States the 'product administered is not the product tested' conclusion -- the marketed Process 2 product differed from the Process 1 trial material on which 95% efficacy was based.

references.quote: The latest protocol written by Pfizer in September 2022, 20th amendment removed this objective “as of July 3, 2022, over 3.6 billion doses of BNT162b2 have been distributed, with over 1.4 billion doses of BNT162b2 administered worldwide, which were manufactured via "Process 2". Given the number of doses now administered worldwide, the originally planned comparison of manufacturing processes is no longer warranted.”
references.relevance: Documents the protocol amendment (20th amendment, Sept 2022) that removed the Process 1/Process 2 comparison objective, directly relevant to the 'Process 2 amendment' document cited in the todo.

findings.summary: The book directly recounts the October 2022 European Parliament special COVI hearing where Janine Small (standing in for Albert Bourla) was forced to admit transmission was never studied, and provides the Parliament webstreaming URL in a footnote.

references.quote: Albert Bourla, who had been summoned to the European Parliament, did not deign to attend, leaving his place to Janine Small, in charge of the laboratory’s international markets. The hearing can still be viewed on the Parliament’s website241.
references.relevance: Identifies the same 10 Oct 2022 EP COVI hearing and Janine Small testimony; footnote 241 gives the Parliament's official webstreaming link (multimedia.europarl.europa.eu .../special-committee-on-COVID-19-pandemic_202210101430-COMMITTEE-COVI), which is exactly the source the follow-up asks to locate/link.

references.quote: Rob Roos, on the other hand, “forced” poor Janine Small, who was sent to the coalface by her director, to publicly admit that transmission had never been studied in the clinical trial, which was far from being a revelation for me as a biostatistician, since we had been aware of it since December 2020242 !!!!
references.relevance: This is precisely the moment cited in the interview — Pfizer's Small publicly admitting transmission was not tested; footnote 242 also links Rob Roos's tweet of the exchange.

references.quote: no evidence of efficacy against asymptomatic infection, against the long-term effects of COVID-19 disease, against the transmission of SARS-CoV-2, or against mortality
references.relevance: Corroborates the substance of the transmission gap from Pfizer's own April 2021 documentation, showing Cotton knew transmission was never demonstrated long before Small's public admission.

findings.summary: The book devotes a full section to Augusto Roux's case for the trial-injury argument, quoting his EMA complaint email and his medical documents, though it does not reproduce the full medical record itself (which the follow-up asks to add to the archive).

references.quote: "My name is Augusto German Roux, 36 years old, born on August 18, 1984, living in Buenos Aires, Argentina. I wanted to file a complaint regarding the adverse reactions I suffered after volunteering to receive the Pfizer SARSCov2 vaccine at the Cosme Argerich Central Military Hospital in the city of Buenos Aires. After the second dose, I had fever spikes of more than 40 degrees (which lasted for several weeks), I suffered from loss of consciousness, fainting, tachycardia that almost left me for dead, my urine was extremely dark, etc."
references.relevance: Reproduces Roux's own account/complaint of his trial injuries — the core content of the medical record the follow-up wants archived; footnotes 85/86 (davidhealy.org/disappeared-in-argentina; vaccineharm.wordpress.com) point to where his documents were made public.

references.quote: Augusto and Brianne have published an article together, stating that four doctors examined Augusto and confirmed that his injuries were vaccine-induced88. Due to his health issues, Augusto discontinued his participation in the Pfizer clinical trial. However, the database records that he left the trial for personal reasons, not due to serious adverse events related to the vaccine.
references.relevance: Documents the medical-record-vs-database discrepancy at the heart of the trial-injury argument; footnote 88 links a PubMed article (pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36710689) — a citable source for the estate request.

references.quote: The Argentinean center staff even added a non-existent suspected case of COVID-19 and an imaginary anxiety disorder to his record. Augusto has proof to the contrary.
references.relevance: Confirms Cotton's claim that she/Roux possess the underlying record evidence ("proof to the contrary"), matching the interview statement that she has the full record.

findings.summary: The book explicitly names the Senator Ron Johnson Senate hearing/press conference where Maddie de Garay's case (and Brianne Dressen's) was presented, which is the recording the follow-up wants to locate and catalog.

references.quote: As I was poking around, I stumbled upon an interview with Senator Ron Johnson of Wisconsin in a grand Senate chamber. He generously gave the floor to vaccine victims. Intrigued, I added English subtitles to ensure I grasped everything because a woman spoke passionately about a young girl seated in a wheelchair beside her. Here is what I call the case of Maddie de Garay.
references.relevance: Directly identifies the Ron Johnson Senate event where Maddie de Garay's case was presented — exactly the recording to be located and added to the catalog/external_links.

references.quote: The final tipping point for my passiveness and silence was crossing paths with a brave young girl named Maddie de Garay a vibrant, thriving, and healthy 12year-old who was severely injured in the Pfizer clinical trial. We met in person at a press conference by Senator Ron Johnson and clicked right away.
references.relevance: Brianne Dressen's account (quoted in the book) confirms a Senator Ron Johnson press conference as the venue where Maddie was presented, reinforcing the specific event to catalog.

references.quote: She was heard by the US Senator Ron Johnson234, her story encapsulates the discrediting of many victims by trolls and fact-checkers of various persuasions.
references.relevance: Footnote 234 attaches a citable source for the Ron Johnson hearing where these victims (de Garay, Dressen) testified, useful for the external_links entry.

findings.summary: This is a maintenance/cross-linking task rather than a research question, but the book confirms the relevant cited documents (the EP COVI hearing and the Pfizer C4591001 protocol) and provides their canonical URLs, supporting cross-linking to external_links.html.

references.quote: The hearing can still be viewed on the Parliament’s website241.
references.relevance: Footnote 241 supplies the EP COVI hearing's official Parliament webstreaming URL — the authoritative link to use when cross-linking the interview brief to external_links.html.

references.quote: “In addition to the C4591001 trial, Pfizer plans to evaluate in other studies:
references.relevance: Shows the book cites the Pfizer C4591001 protocol; footnotes 25/128/243 all give the canonical Pfizer protocol URL (cdn.pfizer.com/.../C4591001_Clinical_Protocol_Nov2020.pdf), the link to reference in external_links for cross-linking.

findings.summary: The book directly addresses the antipyretic confounding argument and states the vaccine group used far more antipyretics, biasing the 95% efficacy figure, though it does not state the precise 3.5x figure or cite a specific interim-CSR data table.

references.quote: Another problem is the permitted use of antipyretics, fever-reducing drugs, in case of a reaction right after the injections of each dose. These drugs may have actually suppressed fevers that are a symptom of COVID. You may have heard of people who were vaccinated and became ill within a few days of a dose. So using antipyretics means fewer cases of COVID in the trial.
references.relevance: Establishes the mechanism Cotton describes in the interview: antipyretics suppress COVID symptoms, so fewer cases are tested/counted, inflating efficacy.

references.quote: Well, it is a big deal when you know that in the results, the vaccinated people used much more antipyretics than the saltwater vaccinated people did. This constitutes what we call a statistical bias, i.e., an element likely to make the results deviate from their true value.
references.relevance: Direct match to the interview claim that the vaccine arm used far more antipyretics than placebo, biasing the result; this is the imbalance the 3.5x figure refers to (exact ratio not given in book).

references.quote: Regarding the intake of antipyretics, it was possible to provide COVID cases for the subgroup of participants who had completed the reactogenicity diary.
references.relevance: Connects antipyretic use to the reactogenicity-diary data, the source from which the antipyretic-use imbalance in the trial is derived.

findings.summary: The book discusses the EU vaccine procurement (von der Leyen-Bourla SMS contracts for billions of doses/euros) but does not state the specific 4.2 billion doses figure, the Advance Purchase Agreements as a document, or that the orders proved boosters were anticipated.

references.quote: Ms. Rivasi had been diligently working for months to uncover the details of the orders placed via text message by Ms. Von Der Leyen, President of the European Commission, to Pfizer CEO Albert Bourla. For those who may not be aware, it is worth noting that the vaccine vials for all member states were ordered via text message, a highly unusual procedure, especially considering the substantial order amounting to several billion euros.
references.relevance: Addresses the EU procurement contracts at the heart of the todo, though framed via the von der Leyen-Bourla SMS scandal rather than the formal Advance Purchase Agreements or a 4.2 billion doses count.

references.quote: When I think about the amount of paperwork that every subcontractor in the pharmaceutical industry has to sign, ordering billions of doses by SMS is almost a joke.
references.relevance: References the scale of EU dose orders ('billions of doses'), the closest the book comes to the 4.2 billion doses procurement figure cited in the interview.

findings.summary: The book directly and repeatedly addresses the December 2020 booster sub-study, treating it as evidence Pfizer already knew protection would wane at the moment of authorization.

references.quote: Why was a booster study planned as early as December 2020 when we were informed that two doses would enable a return to normal life? Did they know that immune protection only lasted a few months? Did they intend to conceal their rapid decline?
references.relevance: Direct match: the book makes exactly the interview argument that a booster study planned in December 2020 proves Pfizer was preparing boosters at the time of initial authorization.

references.quote: Need for a booster dose: based on experience with adults, it is likely that a booster dose will be needed in addition to the three-dose primary series to increase
references.relevance: Quotes trial-report language anticipating a booster dose, corroborating that booster planning was baked into the program early.

references.quote: it submitted a request to the FDA for approval of a booster dose. This third dose
references.relevance: Documents the booster's progression to an FDA submission, part of the booster-substudy narrative Cotton raises in the interview.

findings.summary: The book does not address von der Leyen's meetings with Bill Gates, Global Citizen, or Open Society Foundations, nor the EU Transparency Register; its von der Leyen content is confined to the Pfizer SMS-contract transparency dispute.

The book cites a McKinsey article on accelerated ('100-day') vaccine development but does not address the specific EU Commission-McKinsey 2.5 million euro medical-countermeasures contract referenced in the interview.

references.quote: there is now serious discussion of what it will take to cut the time from sequence to authorization to just 100 days for the next emerging threat.
references.relevance: Quotes the McKinsey article (footnote link to mckinsey.com) Cotton references, but this is the accelerated-development 'ten lessons' piece, not the EU-McKinsey countermeasures contract the todo asks for; included to show the only McKinsey-EU touchpoint in the book.

findings.summary: The book addresses this very directly: it dedicates a passage to 'page 38' of the April 2021 adolescent (12-15) clinical report and lists the exact unknowns Cotton cites (duration of protection, no efficacy in high-risk populations, previously infected, asymptomatic infection, long-term effects, transmission, mortality).

references.quote:
In this same report, I observe a lack of efficacy in severe cases, akin to findings for adults, particularly on page 38.
Ah, page 38! Dear readers, brace yourselves because what follows may make you fall off your chairs!
In April 2021, the table on page 38 revealed that the duration of protection was unknown for adolescents aged 12 to 15 years.
references.relevance: Names the exact document (April 2021 adolescent 12-15 report) and the exact page (38) Cotton points to in the interview, and confirms the unknown duration of protection.

references.quote:
Pfizer graciously informed us that there was
•
•
•
no evidence of efficacy of the vaccine in certain populations at high risk of severe COVID-19,
no evidence of efficacy in individuals previously infected with SARSCoV-2,
no evidence of efficacy against asymptomatic infection, against the long-term effects of COVID-19 disease, against the transmission of SARS-CoV-2, or against mortality
... and this applies not only to adolescents but also to adults!
references.relevance: Lists in black and white the same unknowns Cotton enumerates in the interview: at-risk populations, symptomatic/asymptomatic cases, long-term effects, transmission, and mortality.

references.quote: They also highlighted the unproven efficacy in severe cases and the small size of the adolescent trial in April 2021, which aligns perfectly with my own conclusions.
references.relevance: Reinforces the page-38 unknowns by noting the French Ethics Committee independently flagged unproven efficacy and the small adolescent trial size.

findings.summary: Strongly addressed: the book quotes the CHMP Assessment Report of 19 February 2021 on Process 1 (clinical) vs Process 2 (commercial), the drop in full-length RNA (69-81% to ~59%), the EMA's December 2020 irregularities exposed by the cyber-attack, the 'MAJOR OBJECTION' classification, and ties it to the Process 2 protocol amendment (250 participants).

references.quote: Concerning manufacturing process, EMA had noted some irregularities as early as December 9, 2020. This case has reached us, not thanks to the communication of health authorities but following a cyber-attack!
references.relevance: Documents EMA's pre-authorization reservations on the manufacturing process (the Process 2 / mRNA-integrity concern the todo asks for).

references.quote:
In clinical trials, the product administered contained between 69% and 81% of full-length RNA, while the vials manufactured in the new production lines contained only 59% on average.
A "sticking point," for the EMA in November 2020 according to the documents leaked.
The Committee for Medicinal Products for Human Use (CHMP) Assessment report of the 19 February 2021 states that "two manufacturing processes for the active substance were used during the development history: process 1 (clinical trial material) and process 2 (commercial process)"
references.relevance: Quotes the EMA/CHMP assessment report directly on the mRNA-integrity (full-length RNA) discrepancy and explicitly distinguishes Process 1 vs Process 2.

references.quote:
EMA classified these issued as a MAJOR OBJECTION299.
Major objection leading to an authorization? Great job guys!
references.relevance: Confirms the EMA assessment report flagged the Process 2 issues as a 'major objection,' exactly the EPAR passage the todo seeks; the following lines tie it to the protocol's 250-participant Process 2 comparison.

findings.summary: Addressed: the book attributes the under-reporting / multiply-by-up-to-100 figure to a 2006 article by the head of France's Pharmacovigilance Centres (named in the book as 'Jonville-Bera,' apparently the same source the todo transcribes as 'Bérat'), supporting Cotton's 10x-100x multiplier.

references.quote: It was impossible to believe that all this was just random noise in the statistics, especially when it's well known that the number of reported cases represents only 1 to 10% of the actual cases. Mrs. Jonville-Bera, Head of the Pharmacovigilance Centers in France, wrote this in a 2006 article on vaccine adverse events38! This means that, in 'real life,' I had to multiply my numbers by at least 10, or even up to 100!
references.relevance: Directly cites the 2006 French pharmacovigilance under-reporting article (footnote 38: pubmed 16343870) and the 'multiply by up to 100' figure the todo describes; the author is given as Jonville-Bera (likely the 'Jean-Ville Bérat' mishearing).

references.quote: 5,500 deaths multiplied by 100—that is over half a million deaths! That is between 3 and 5 times the number of victims of the Hiroshima atomic bomb, according to various estimates.
references.relevance: Shows Cotton applying the under-reporting multiplier (x100) drawn from the 2006 publication, the empirical basis the todo identifies.

findings.summary: Not meaningfully addressed: the book discusses waning efficacy and the booster cascade thematically but does not contain the specific ANSM February 2023 update figures (45% post-primovaccination, 56% after the 3rd dose, 75% after the 4th-5th doses, decaying to 22% at 6 months) that Bercoff read in the interview intro.

The book does not address the leaked Pfizer-South Africa supply contract (April 2021) or its page-8 declaration that Phase 2-3 trials were 'completed'; it mentions South Africa only as one of the trial sites and discusses U.S. Department of Defense supply contracts, not the Health Justice Initiative / South Africa disclosure.

references.quote: All the interim analyses upon which the marketing authorizations (issued in December 2020, April 2021, October 2021, and June 2022) were based, could only be conducted on participants followed up for a maximum of 3 months, thanks to a new regulation specific to anti-COVID vaccines hastily drafted by the FDA in response to the emergency. However, this very short follow-up time is entirely insufficient to evaluate the medium- and long-term safety, which remains unknown.
references.relevance: Directly describes the regulatory pivot the todo calls the FDA 'freeway' — a new FDA rule for COVID vaccines that legalized authorizations based on only ~3 months / two months of follow-up — though the book does not name the June 2020 'Guidance on Development and Licensure of Vaccines to Prevent COVID-19' by title.

references.quote: It is worth noting that it is precisely this change in recommendations that conveniently allowed the laboratory to avoid presenting the antibody assay at 6 months after the second dose. Without this alteration, they would have been compelled to reconsider their revolutionary biotechnology, as no health agency would have been willing to grant authorization for a vaccine whose protection wanes in such a short period.
references.relevance: Explains the consequence of the FDA's shortened-follow-up rule — the same 'freeway' argument Cotton makes in the interview.

references.quote: It is important to note that the 95% efficacy results presented to the world were based on an interim analysis after a maximum of three months of follow-up, i.e., M3, with the trial expected to last two years (24 months).
references.relevance: States the truncated-follow-up point (three months vs. the two-year trial) that the June 2020 FDA guidance enabled, matching Cotton's claim that efficacy was declared on far less than two years of data.

findings.summary: The book addresses the same regulatory pivot — an FDA rule that allowed COVID-vaccine authorizations on roughly two-to-three months of follow-up instead of the full two-year trial — but does not cite the June 2020 FDA guidance document by its title or date.

references.quote: The results of the preclinical study on macaques corroborate the emerging conclusions, as a decline in neutralizing antibodies was also noted at 2 months after the second dose132.
references.relevance: Cites the pre-clinical macaque immunogenicity publication (footnote 132 links to the bioRxiv paper) showing antibody waning at two months — the exact document referenced in the todo.

references.quote: This was the information conveyed by Philippe Douste-Blazy, former French Minister of Health and former Deputy Secretary-General of the United Nations, during an interview with France Info on November 9, 2020133. “If there is a vaccine, that’s great news, but we have to be careful. The antibodies that Pfizer’s vaccine produces start to decline after two months”.
references.relevance: Matches the substance of the todo (a French health-establishment figure publicly warning on France Info that Pfizer antibodies last only a few months), but the book attributes this warning to Philippe Douste-Blazy, not to Jérôme Salomon as the interview transcript states — likely a misattribution in the interview.

references.quote: The absence of neutralizing antibody assays beyond two months after dose 2 allowed for the omission of reporting the rapid waning of vaccine protection. This oversight is particularly egregious since this decline was already observable and hence predictable in preclinical results on macaques.
references.relevance: Reinforces that the macaque preclinical data foreshadowed antibody waning, the basis for the public warning the todo asks about.

findings.summary: The book addresses the antibody-waning warning and cites the pre-clinical macaque publication, but attributes the November 9, 2020 France Info warning to Philippe Douste-Blazy rather than Jérôme Salomon.

references.quote: On April 5, 2022, at around 9:00 a.m., I joined the teleconference organized by the OPECST. During the session, I presented my conclusions regarding the Pfizer clinical trial, which You’re now familiar with. As I wrapped up my presentation on the inaccuracies in efficacy, I remarked, “I’m not sure if you’ve had the chance to review my report, but I’ve certainly read yours”.
references.relevance: Confirms and dates Cotton's OPECST hearing (April 5, 2022), the formal record the todo asks to verify.

references.quote: The rapporteurs began their cycle of hearings on March 28. I was honored to be among the people auditioned. The people representing the State were the health authorities, the Director General of the ANSM as well as some of her collaborators, the President of the Technical Commission on Vaccinations at the Haute Autorité de Santé...
references.relevance: Documents the OPECST hearing process and that the ANSM Director General was among those heard; provides context for the institutional channels in the todo.

findings.summary: The book confirms Cotton's OPECST hearing (April 5, 2022) and discusses the ANSM's involvement in the OPECST hearings, but does not describe a separate July 2022 closed/in-camera ANSM hearing of Cotton herself.

references.quote: He proceeded to share his story and that of his son, Maxime.
references.relevance: Introduces the Maxime case that the todo asks to document.

references.quote: That Monday, July 26, 2021, I was having lunch in one of my restaurants... my son, told me that he had an appointment at 2 pm to be vaccinated against COVID-19 because he wanted to go to Greece with his girlfriend.
references.relevance: Establishes Maxime as the 22-year-old vaccinated at 2 pm on July 26, 2021 — the case in the todo.

references.quote: I don’t know it yet, but on the same day, a newspaper published the following statement , “Nine hours after receiving an injection from Pfizer, a 22-year-old died...” ... He went so far as to say that my son’s death was fictitious... the name Maxime Beltra. ... The autopsy report, available in October, concluded that there is no evidence to suggest the death was the result of anaphylaxis...
references.relevance: Names the victim (Maxime Beltra), confirms death the same day as the 2 pm vaccination, and references the autopsy report — matching the todo, though the book gives the interval as ~9-10 hours (not 5-6) and does not mention the 'wrong arm examined' autopsy detail.

findings.summary: The book fully documents the Maxime (Beltra) case — a 22-year-old vaccinated at 2 pm on July 26, 2021 who died the same evening, with an autopsy report cited — but states the interval as roughly nine to ten hours rather than 5-6 hours and does not include the 'wrong arm examined' detail.

The book directly and richly addresses the LinkedIn suspension and the institutional visitor log, naming the US Department of State, WHO, French ministries, and the 133 lawyers, plus the censorship timeline.

references.quote: The next day, I shared it on the LinkedIn professional network with my former contacts in the pharmaceutical industry to get their opinion on my work. The reception was mostly positive. My publication was censored by LinkedIn within 24 hours. I reposted it and told the moderators they had no right to censor it since I did not call for violence, nor did I make raging or discriminatory remarks. Unfortunately, my second post was censored within the hour!
references.relevance: Confirms the exact LinkedIn censorship timeline Cotton describes: original post, 24-hour censorship, repost, then second censorship within the hour.

references.quote: My illustrious visitors worked at Thales, Apple, TotalEnergies, Airbus, the Ministry of Defense, the French National Center for Scientific Research (CNRS), the Ministry of the Armed Forces, the French Police, and even the World Health Organization; They were consultants, business strategists, or even lawyers, not only in France, but also in Belgium. They worked for the Canton of Geneva in Switzerland, as well as at the U.S. Department of State.
references.relevance: This is the visit log itself - naming the WHO, French ministries, the US Department of State, and others - exactly the institutional visitors the todo asks to document.

references.quote: 133 lawyers looked at my profile in a few days. Unusual. Almost disturbing, but not one contacted me, so I continued.
references.relevance: Documents the unusual surge of lawyer visits to her LinkedIn profile after she posted the January 2022 report.

findings.summary: The book explicitly documents both the OPECST hearing (April 5, 2022) and the ANSM hearing (July 2022) as the formal channels through which Cotton, in her whistleblower role, alerted French authorities.

references.quote: On April 5, 2022, at around 9:00 a.m., I joined the teleconference organized by the OPECST. During the session, I presented my conclusions regarding the Pfizer clinical trial, which You’re now familiar with. As I wrapped up my presentation on the inaccuracies in efficacy, I remarked, “I’m not sure if you’ve had the chance to review my report, but I’ve certainly read yours”.
references.relevance: Documents the April 2022 OPECST hearing where Cotton formally presented her critique to the parliamentary office.

references.quote: In light of all these elements, now more than ever, I stand by my conclusions from January 2022. These conclusions were presented during my private hearing in April 2022 with the Parliamentary Office of Scientific and Technological Choices and certain members of the French Health Agency (ANSM) in July 2022.
references.relevance: Directly names both the April 2022 OPECST hearing and the July 2022 ANSM hearing as the formal channels for her January 2022 conclusions - exactly the two records the todo asks to cross-reference.

references.quote: Since April 2021, I’ve been using my skills as a biostatistician to serve the community—for free, mind you. Some call it conspiracy, others see it as heroism.
references.relevance: Establishes Cotton's self-described whistleblower role (the section is headed THE WHISTLEBLOWER) that frames her use of the OPECST and ANSM channels.

findings.summary: The book devotes substantial space to fact-checkers and the 'conspiracy' labeling apparatus but does NOT mention the Fonds Marianne, Conspiracy Watch, or any Senate hearing disclosing a public-subsidy share (~50%) for a fact-checking organization.

The book mentions hydroxychloroquine being subjected to severe regulation and discrediting campaigns, and references the Mediator affair as an ANSM regulatory failure, but it does NOT document the ANSM's refusal of Raoult's ATU/RTU request or the argument that the post-Mediator ATU/RTU framework was designed for such off-label use; Raoult is not named and the ATU/RTU framework is not discussed.

The book directly addresses the recalculation: using Aaron Siri's publicly released data and the anti-nucleocapsid (non-S seroconversion) serology endpoint, Cotton recomputes vaccine efficacy at approximately 53% rather than 95% (the interview cites ~55%).

references.quote: Upon recalculating based on the serological criterion using the data made publicly available by Mr. Siri, I’ve determined that the efficacy of the vaccine on ALL COVID cases, and not solely on mild or moderate symptomatic cases confirmed by PCR tests, is approximately 53%! This is significantly lower and, therefore, not as promising as the initially announced 95% efficacy.
references.relevance: The core recalculation Cotton describes at ~16:25 — anti-nucleocapsid serology endpoint yields ~53% (interview: ~55%) instead of the 95% headline, driven by the choice of the PCR-confirmed primary endpoint.

references.quote: Furthermore, this criterion fails to represent the full spectrum of the disease, as efficacy calculated based on anti-nucleocapsid serology, also known as non-S-seroconversion to SARS-CoV-2 infection, which includes all COVID cases, is approximately 53%, calculated using the SAS® database.
references.relevance: Restates the recomputed efficacy figure and names the SAS database as the data source — the court-released Pfizer C4591001 dataset the todo asks to reproduce from.

references.quote: An anti-nucleocapsid serology, detecting antibodies produced in response to viral illness, was conducted at every planned visit, the results were never presented during each Vaccines and Related Biological Products Advisory Committee Meeting.
references.relevance: Establishes that the anti-nucleocapsid serology endpoint underlying the recalculation existed in the trial but was withheld from the VRBPAC presentations — the case-definition argument.

findings.summary: The book addresses the Pfizer pregnancy trial (NCT04754594, started Feb 2021) in detail, noting Pfizer's prolonged delay and incomplete results; it nuances the interview claim, stating results were eventually published (July 2023 and finally July 2024) but were too incomplete to conclude on efficacy in pregnant women.

references.quote: In order to ensure reliable data, the Pfizer laboratory set up a trial as early as February 9, 2021 with the aim of studying possible adverse effects on the patients themselves but also on the babies. In February 2022, Pfizer revealed that they still did not have "a complete set of data." It was not until July 13, 2023, that Pfizer published the results and the study documents.
references.relevance: Identifies the Pfizer pregnancy trial Cotton references at ~12:00 (NCT04754594) and the timeline of withheld/delayed results that the todo asks to document.

references.quote: Finally, despite the one-year delay between the end of the trial and the publication of results on the clinical trial website, the results submitted in October 2023, which we have just reviewed, are very incomplete. The number of COVID cases and the results of neutralizing antibody titers were missing. Pfizer has therefore taken a year to publish results that do not allow for any conclusions regarding the vaccine’s efficacy in pregnant women and present erroneous tolerance results. The missing results were finally published in July 2024.
references.relevance: Directly supports and refines Cotton's interview statement that results were not released — the book says they were delayed/incomplete and only fully published in July 2024, with no usable efficacy conclusion in pregnant women.

references.quote: The document clearly states in the Summary of Data/Rationale for Risk” table that: "There are no ongoing COVID-19 vaccine studies including pregnant women and, therefore, the potential for adverse obstetric and/or neonatal outcomes following vaccination while pregnant is unknown."
references.relevance: Documents Pfizer's own statement (in the pregnancy study protocol) that obstetric/neonatal risks were unknown — supporting the todo's point that an unpublished/incomplete pregnancy trial undermines 'no signal' assurances.

findings.summary: The book captures the same regulatory caveat — that the data did not confirm efficacy on severe forms, asymptomatic forms, or the over-75 population — though it cites the French VIDAL/Comirnaty wording (Feb 2021) and the Moderna/AstraZeneca conclusions rather than naming the HAS 2 February 2021 AZD1222 opinion specifically.

references.quote: However, the results presented cannot confirm the vaccine’s efficacy against severe forms of COVID19, asymptomatic forms, or in individuals over 75 years old. Nevertheless, they do not rule out its efficacy, as more data is needed for definitive confirmation.
references.relevance: Near-verbatim match to the HAS-style caveat Bercoff reads (~15:17) that data do not allow conclusions on efficacy in the elderly, severe forms, or asymptomatic forms — here quoted from the Feb 2021 VIDAL entry on Comirnaty.

references.quote: Regarding efficacy on severe cases, the conclusions were identical for both Moderna and AstraZeneca vaccines: no statistically demonstrated efficacy in clinical trials.
references.relevance: Directly addresses the AstraZeneca (AZD1222) efficacy conclusion the todo references — no statistically demonstrated efficacy on severe forms, a French-regulator counterweight to the 95% messaging.

references.quote: The results presented in the various reports spanning from 6-month-old babies to elderly individuals do not provide conclusive evidence of statistically proven efficacy in preventing severe COVID cases at the time of emergency use authorization or marketing authorizations... As of December 2020, efficacy in the over-75 population had not been substantiated.
references.relevance: Cotton's own conclusion confirming no proven efficacy on severe forms or the over-75 group at authorization — the substance behind the HAS-opinion citation requested.

findings.summary: The book does not contain the radio call-in segments (Emilien, a suspended firefighter; Christophe of Angers) from this Sud Radio broadcast; these are live-interview anecdotes with no counterpart in the book text.

The book directly and extensively addresses the Argentine military-hospital site, naming the Cosme Argerich Central Military Hospital in Buenos Aires, the >5700 participants at that single site, and the Augusto Roux fraud allegation.

references.quote: The Argentinean site that recruited Augusto Roux included more than 5700 participants between April 2020 and November 2020, and this out of a total of 40,000.
references.relevance: Confirms the single Argentine site held ~5700 of 40,000 participants, exactly the ~15% concentration the todo cites.

references.quote: "My name is Augusto German Roux, 36 years old, born on August 18, 1984, living in Buenos Aires, Argentina. I wanted to file a complaint regarding the adverse reactions I suffered after volunteering to receive the Pfizer SARSCov2 vaccine at the Cosme Argerich Central Military Hospital in the city of Buenos Aires."
references.relevance: Names the military hospital and Roux, the concrete entry point into the site identified in the todo.

references.quote: However, the database records that he left the trial for personal reasons, not due to serious adverse events related to the vaccine. The Argentinean center staff even added a non-existent suspected case of COVID-19 and an imaginary anxiety disorder to his record. Augusto has proof to the contrary. We are clearly dealing with blatant fraud intended to conceal serious effects that posed a life-threatening risk.
references.relevance: States the audit/fraud allegation at the Argentine center directly tied to the Roux case the todo seeks to document.

findings.summary: The book covers Brooke ("Brook") Jackson and Ventavia Research Group at length, including the FDA complaint, the data-falsification allegations, the BMJ article, and the ongoing litigation.

references.quote: Ventavia Research Group hired Brook Jackson, a 20-year veteran of clinical trial coordination and management, as a regional manager152 on September 7, 2020, to oversee the company’s clinical trial operations, recruitment, and quality assurance. In this capacity, she was involved in the Pfizer/BioNtech Phase 2/3 trial. Brook, along with two other employees who wished to remain anonymous, have accused Ventavia of several infractions, including falsifying data, employing inadequately trained staff, delaying follow-up on reported adverse events, failing to complete data entry promptly, and neglecting to conduct PCR testing on all participants with COVID-19-like symptoms
references.relevance: Identifies Jackson and the Ventavia GCP-violation allegations central to the todo.

references.quote: She shared her account in an article in the British Medical Journal153, circulated worldwide under the title “Pfizer Gate”, yet received no coverage in our mainstream media.
references.relevance: References the BMJ whistleblower article (Thacker, Nov 2021) the todo wants linked; footnote 153 is the BMJ URL (bmj.com/content/375/bmj.n2635).

references.quote: Brook Jackson, medical director for Ventavia, testifies to the serious violations of Good Clinical Practices that she witnessed. This casts doubt on the reliability and integrity of the entire trial data and calls into question the entire results. Since January 2021, Brook’s legal battle against Ventavia and Pfizer has been raging. All documents in the case are openly available for the world to follow the progress of the case272.
references.relevance: Documents the False Claims Act litigation status the todo seeks; footnote 272 points to iambrookjackson.com case documents.

findings.summary: The book references the French criminal complaint for poisoning filed against "unknown parties," and links Cotton's expertise/methodological analysis to these legal actions, though without full filing-party/dismissal details.

references.quote: In France, as soon as May 2022, several victims have already filed a complaint against “unknown parties” for poisoning. Many legal actions are ongoing against Pfizer but obtaining formal recognition of a link between vaccination and death is a real assault course.
references.page: Page 175 (nearest marker; passage appears between Page 174 and Page 176 markers)
references.relevance: Directly documents the French criminal complaint for poisoning (plainte contre X / 'unknown parties'), the legal vehicle the todo describes, with the May 2022 filing date.

references.quote: In March 2024, Augusto Roux officially initiated a criminal investigation in Argentina against Fernando Polack, the principal investigator of the Argentine center, and Pfizer, for falsification of public documents and abandonment of a person.
references.relevance: A parallel criminal action (Argentina) tied to the same fraud evidence; useful context for the legal-actions track even though it is not the French complaint itself.

findings.summary: The book cites a February 2024 peer-reviewed paper showing spike protein crossing the fetal-placental barrier and repeatedly documents the absence of pregnancy-safety data and biodistribution beyond the injection site, matching the todo precisely.

references.quote: In February 2024, a scientific paper reported on two pregnant women who were vaccinated a few days before delivery. The authors found the spike protein in the placental tissue, demonstrating that the COVID-19 mRNA vaccine is capable of penetrating the fetal-placental barrier and reaching the intrauterine environment
references.relevance: Exactly the peer-reviewed placental-transfer publication the todo asks to identify; footnote 256 gives the ScienceDirect URL (S0002937824000632).

references.quote: The manufacturer’s pharmacokinetic studies show that the Lipid Nano Particles (LNP) are distributed mainly in the muscle at the injection site and in the liver. According to a confidential Pfizer document obtained through the FOIA, the LNP were found in the ovaries of female rats101. Subsequent publications have shown that they transport mRNA throughout the body and are found in the heart, brain, and other organs
references.relevance: Documents biodistribution beyond the injection site (ovaries, organs), the mechanistic basis for the placental-transfer / pregnancy-safety concern.

references.quote: Finally, despite the one-year delay between the end of the trial and the publication of results on the clinical trial website, the results submitted in October 2023, which we have just reviewed, are very incomplete... Pfizer has therefore taken a year to publish results that do not allow for any conclusions regarding the vaccine’s efficacy in pregnant women and present erroneous tolerance results.
references.relevance: Exposes the gap between official pregnancy recommendations and the absence of adequate pregnancy-safety data, which the todo asks to cross-link.

findings.summary: The book confirms a central/local PCR-lab arrangement existed and that vaccine-arm participants were tested less often (a detection bias), but it does not document the specific Pearl River as authoritative-over-positive-local-test arrangement described by Pierre.

references.quote: Request that Pfizer provide files tracking participants' phone calls, callbacks by the center, and PCR test results from both local and central laboratories.
references.relevance: Directly references the existence of both local and central PCR laboratories in C4591001 — the structural feature Pierre describes — though Cotton frames it as a missing record to be requested rather than detailing the Pearl-River-overrides-local-test rule.

references.quote: Moreover, we have confirmation in the database that participants who received the vaccine were tested less frequently than those in the placebo group, which introduced a major bias in counting the number of COVID-19 cases, the primary efficacy criterion, in favor of the vaccine
references.relevance: Documents the case-detection bias arising from the PCR-testing arrangement, which is the structural consequence Pierre links to the central-vs-local lab setup.

references.quote: the failure to follow up on the participants who reported suffering from adverse events
references.relevance: Part of Cotton's list of data-handling failures tied to PCR-assay performance for suspected COVID cases, the same testing pathway the central/local lab arrangement governs.

findings.summary: The book extensively discusses the Argentine military-hospital site and cites OpenVAET's court-released-database audit, including the Roux case, but it does not state the specific figure of 301 disappeared subjects or the 17-subject same-day enrollment cluster.

references.quote: Thanks to documents made public by a court decision, several whistleblowers have been examining the files containing data reported by each participant's site for over a year now.
references.relevance: Describes the very effort (court-released database, whistleblowers including Pierre/OpenVAET) that produced the 301-disappeared-subjects finding the todo seeks to archive.

references.quote: The Argentinean site that recruited Augusto Roux included more than 5700 participants between April 2020 and November 2020, and this out of a total of 40,000. 10,000 participants were recruited across only 5 centers.
references.relevance: Documents the Argentina enrollment concentration central to the OpenVAET analysis of clustered/disappeared subjects around Roux's site.

references.quote: The Argentinean center staff even added a non-existent suspected case of COVID-19 and an imaginary anxiety disorder to his record. Augusto has proof to the contrary. We are clearly dealing with blatant fraud intended to conceal serious effects that posed a life-threatening risk.
references.relevance: Covers the Roux-site data-fabrication finding from the released database; the OpenVAET Substack (cited at footnote 150) is the same source documenting the disappeared/clustered subjects.

findings.summary: The book directly addresses the leaked EMA-Pfizer correspondence and the Process 1 vs Process 2 mRNA-integrity discrepancy, quoting the CHMP assessment report and the EMA 'major objection' classification.

references.quote: In clinical trials, the product administered contained between 69% and 81% of full-length RNA, while the vials manufactured in the new production lines contained only 59% on average. A "sticking point," for the EMA in November 2020 according to the documents leaked.
references.relevance: This is exactly the mRNA-integrity discrepancy between Process 1 (trial) and Process 2 (marketed) product flagged in the December 2020 EMA hack/leak that the todo seeks to archive.

references.quote: The Committee for Medicinal Products for Human Use (CHMP) Assessment report of the 19 February 2021 states that "two manufacturing processes for the active substance were used during the development history: process 1 (clinical trial material) and process 2 (commercial process)"
references.relevance: Provides the documentary CHMP citation distinguishing Process 1 and Process 2, the heart of Cotton's substitution claim that Pierre independently cross-references.

references.quote: EMA classified these issued as a MAJOR OBJECTION. Major objection leading to an authorization? Great job guys!
references.relevance: Records that the EMA formally flagged the Process-2 manufacturing/integrity issues as a major objection, the regulatory significance of the leaked correspondence.

findings.summary: The book explicitly calls for the C4591001 audit-trail file to resolve hidden data modifications, but it does not name the Firecrest platform or ICON as the data manager to be audited.

references.quote: Ask Pfizer to provide the file tracing the values and dates of data entry and modifications by the various participants in the investigating centers, a file called "Audit-trail" which would make it possible to identify any modification of data intended to mask a failure in the performance of the main tasks of the trial by the investigating sites, such as the non-performance of the PCR assay for suspected cases of COVID-19
references.relevance: Cotton's direct demand for the audit-trail file is the same request Pierre makes ('Who deleted what, when, and why?') to resolve the 301-disappeared-subjects question, though the book does not name Firecrest or ICON.

references.quote: More generally, in January 2022, I requested a full audit of the Pfizer/BioNtech phase 1-2-3 clinical trial, preferably by auditors external to any organization involved in the development and approval of the Comirnaty vaccine, by delegating teams of quality assurers on site to review all records available at the sites
references.relevance: Establishes Cotton's call for an independent audit of all trial records, the broader frame for auditing the data-management chain that Pierre attributes to ICON/Firecrest.

references.quote: The FDA did audit the sites but they admit that they did not check the integrity of the data, which is vital for a reliable database.
references.relevance: Explains why the audit-trail / data-management logs are needed: the existing FDA audit did not verify data integrity, leaving deletion/modification questions open.

findings.summary: The book directly cites the December 2020 HAS opinion and quotes the passage stating Pfizer already planned to evaluate the relevance of a booster, tying it to the deliberate gap in antibody measurement.

references.quote: Thankfully, the laboratory had foreseen this possibility, as it had already planned to assess the “importance of a boost in terms of sustaining the induced immune response” as early as December 2020, as outlined in the opinion of the French National Authority for Health (HAS) at that time!
references.relevance: Explicitly attributes the December 2020 booster-study acknowledgment to the HAS opinion, the smoking-gun source the todo asks to retrieve.

references.quote:
“In addition to the C4591001 trial, Pfizer plans to evaluate in other studies:
- the relevance of boosting the immune response induced
references.relevance: Verbatim quotation from the HAS opinion confirming Pfizer was already studying a booster at authorization — the exact sentence the todo wants quoted (footnote 134 gives the HAS PDF URL).

references.quote: No follow-up visits were scheduled for participants between 1 and 6 months after receiving the second dose injection, neither to measure antibodies nor to collect data on safety, adverse events, blood parameters, or COVID-19 infections.
references.relevance: Documents the missing 3-month-post-dose-2 antibody measurement that is the core of the interview's pull quote about hiding the drop.

findings.summary: The book describes Cotton's VAERS analysis of ~400,000 lines presented in August 2021, including the within-21-days death figure (67%) that pushed her toward dissecting the Pfizer trial.

references.quote: In my small SAS® database—or rather, my biiigggg one— I proceeded to a significant “cleaning” of the 400,000 or so lines.
references.relevance: Confirms the ~400,000-report VAERS dataset the todo references as the basis of her analysis.

references.quote: In my database at that time, I counted around 5,500 deaths. It is not just the number that alarmed me, but the fact that 28% of deaths occurred within 3 days of vaccination. 41% occurred within 7 days of vaccination, and 67% occurred within 21 days of vaccination.
references.relevance: Gives the within-21-days deaths statistic (book states 67%; todo cites ~60%) from the same VAERS analysis the todo wants archived.

references.quote: For nearly three months, I fine-tuned my programs and presented my results in August 2021.
references.relevance: Dates the public presentation of the VAERS analysis (footnote 37 links the FranceSoir “67% des décès… dans les 21” debriefing), the work the todo asks to locate and archive.

findings.summary: The book notes French victims filed poisoning complaints against “unknown parties” in May 2022, but does not describe the liability-reversal-from-states-to-manufacturers mechanism or that the complaint drew on her expertise report.

references.quote: In France, as soon as May 2022, several victims have already filed a complaint against “unknown parties” for poisoning. Many legal actions are ongoing against Pfizer but obtaining formal recognition of a link between vaccination and death is a real assault course.
references.relevance: Confirms the existence of the French criminal poisoning complaint the todo references, though it gives no case reference and does not connect it to her January 2022 expertise report or the liability-shift theory.

findings.summary: The book recounts that after Cotton posted her January 2022 expertise report on LinkedIn it was censored within 24 hours, and her profile drew visits from Apple, the U.S. Department of State, the WHO, and 133 lawyers.

references.quote: My illustrious visitors worked at Thales, Apple, TotalEnergies, Airbus, the Ministry of Defense, the French National Center for Scientific Research (CNRS), the Ministry of the Armed Forces, the French Police, and even the World Health Organization; They were consultants, business strategists, or even lawyers, not only in France, but also in Belgium. They worked for the Canton of Geneva in Switzerland, as well as at the U.S. Department of State.
references.relevance: Lists the institutional visitors (Apple, WHO, U.S. Department of State) the todo's screenshots are meant to substantiate.

references.quote: 133 lawyers looked at my profile in a few days. Unusual. Almost disturbing, but not one contacted me, so I continued.
references.relevance: States the exact “133 lawyers” figure the todo wants the LinkedIn screenshots to document.

references.quote: My publication was censored by LinkedIn within 24 hours. I reposted it and told the moderators they had no right to censor it since I did not call for violence, nor did I make raging or discriminatory remarks. Unfortunately, my second post was censored within the hour!
references.relevance: Corroborates the interview claim that LinkedIn shut down her post/account, the reason she kept the print-screens.

findings.summary: The book directly cites Douste-Blazy's November 9, 2020 France Info interview about antibodies declining after two months, quotes him verbatim, and notes his subsequent disappearance from media.

references.quote: This was the information conveyed by Philippe Douste-Blazy, former French Minister of Health and former Deputy Secretary-General of the United Nations, during an interview with France Info on November 9, 2020133. “If there is a vaccine, that’s great news, but we have to be careful. The antibodies that Pfizer’s vaccine produces start to decline after two months”.
references.relevance: Identifies the exact interview (France Info, Nov 9 2020) and quotes Douste-Blazy's two-month antibody-decline claim the follow-up seeks to locate.

references.quote: Did our dear minister, Mr. Douste-Blazy, make a significant “blunder” by announcing on a mainstream channel the rapid decrease of antibodies? One may wonder, especially considering his complete disappearance from the scene afterward.
references.relevance: Matches the interview's framing of Douste-Blazy as the lone figure who let the cat out of the bag and then disappeared from media.

references.quote: Thankfully, the laboratory had foreseen this possibility, as it had already planned to assess the “importance of a boost in terms of sustaining the induced immune response” as early as December 2020, as outlined in the opinion of the French National Authority for Health (HAS) at that time134!
references.relevance: Corroborates the interview's linked claim that the HAS opinion shows Pfizer was already studying a booster in December 2020.

findings.summary: The book presents the December 2020 Comirnaty RMP safety-issue list and the September 24, 2021 updated RMP that added myocarditis/pericarditis, supplying both endpoints needed for a what-changed-vs-what-didn't comparison (though it uses Dec 2020 and Sept 2021 rather than Nov 2020 and Sept 2022).

references.quote: Anaphylaxis was classified as an important identified risk in December 202048. Illustration 5 EMA - Pfizer - Risk Management Plan of 25 December 2020
references.relevance: Establishes the earlier-version RMP (Dec 2020) baseline of identified/potential risks and missing information for the side-by-side comparison.

references.quote: on September 24, 2021, the Pfizer laboratory cited myo/pericarditis on the first page of its Risk Management Plan: “Rationale for submitting an updated RMP (v 2.3): This Type II variation includes the new important identified risk of myocarditis and pericarditis based on the Signal of Myocarditis, pericarditis for COVID-19 mRNA vaccine63.
references.relevance: Documents the specific item that changed between RMP versions — myocarditis/pericarditis added as a new important identified risk — the core of the comparison table.

references.quote: We learned that, as of December 2020, there were no data of any kind on efficacy or safety for pregnant or breastfeeding women, immunocompromised patients, frail patients with comorbidities, or those suffering from autoimmune or inflammatory diseases. No interaction studies with other vaccines had been performed, and long-term safety was unknown.
references.relevance: Lists the missing-information categories that persisted across RMP versions, supporting the 'nothing was learned' argument the follow-up wants to tabulate.

findings.summary: The book states the under-reporting principle that only 1-10% of adverse events are reported and cites a 2006 article (PubMed 16343870), but attributes the figure to Mrs. Jonville-Bera, Head of the French Pharmacovigilance Centers, rather than to Micallef.

references.quote: especially when it's well known that the number of reported cases represents only 1 to 10% of the actual cases. Mrs. Jonville-Bera, Head of the Pharmacovigilance Centers in France, wrote this in a 2006 article on vaccine adverse events38! This means that, in 'real life,' I had to multiply my numbers by at least 10, or even up to 100!
references.relevance: Provides the exact 1-10% under-reporting figure and the methodological scaling argument the follow-up references, but sources it to Jonville-Bera (2006), not Micallef.

references.quote: 38 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/16343870/
references.relevance: Gives the citation for the under-reporting figure (the 2006 PubMed article), useful for cataloging though it is the Jonville-Bera paper rather than the Micallef paper named in the follow-up.

references.quote: If only 10% of the actual cases are reported, as previously discussed, a quick
references.relevance: Re-applies the 10% under-reporting / order-of-magnitude scaling rule that the follow-up's methodological justification rests on.

findings.summary: The book directly addresses pre-vaccination institutional knowledge of severe UK allergic reactions and the adrenaline-auto-injector warning, though it cites the UK MHRA's 9 December 2020 alert rather than the December 2020 OPECST report named in the todo.

references.quote: As early as December 9, 2020, the British regulatory agency, the MHRA, issued an alert: “Anyone with a history of significant allergic reaction to a vaccine, drug, or food (such as a history of anaphylactoid reaction or those advised to carry an adrenaline auto-injector) should not receive the Pfizer BioNTech vaccine” the regulatory agency said.
references.relevance: This is the cleanest archived early-warning reference: a UK regulator, days into the first-in-the-world rollout, warning that those advised to carry an adrenaline auto-injector should not receive the vaccine — matching the todo's 'UK first-day allergic reactions and the adrenaline-pen warning'.

references.quote: It also stated that "severe allergic reactions had been reported after administration of Pfizer-BioNTech COVID-19 vaccine in mass vaccinations outside of clinical trials," without specifying whether these were allergies to PEG2000, with real-life vaccination beginning in the UK as early as December 1, 2020.
references.relevance: Documents that severe allergic reactions were reported in the very first days of real-life UK vaccination (beginning December 1, 2020), supporting the institutional-knowledge-before-mass-vaccination point.

references.quote: Anaphylaxis was classified as an important identified risk in December 2020.
references.relevance: Confirms that severe allergic reaction (anaphylaxis) was a formally recognized risk in the December 2020 EMA/Pfizer Risk Management Plan, reinforcing that the allergic-reaction danger was known institutionally before mass vaccination.

findings.summary: The book does not name the EPI-PHARE / Epifar study, but it directly makes the same methodological criticism — that limiting the counting of adverse events to within 21 days of injection systematically under-detects long-tail harms — using a CDC June 2021 table as its example.

references.quote: In the United States, during a CDC meeting in June 2021, Tom Shimakuburo presented a table listing adverse events occurring within 21 days after injection
references.relevance: Concrete example of the 21-day adverse-event window the todo describes, used by Cotton to illustrate how real-world/regulatory analyses define an 'adverse event' only within 21 days of injection.

references.quote: Is it reasonable to limit counting adverse events to within 21 days after a single injection? Considering the time required to establish a diagnosis, to have an autopsy result, it would have been more prudent to adjust the usual standards to ensure that no serious signals were overlooked, wouldn’t it?
references.relevance: States Cotton's exact methodological objection — the same point she makes in the interview about real-world studies under-detecting long-tail harms by capping the window at 21 days.

references.quote: Even worse, so-called retrospective studies conducted on existing databases have the lowest level of evidence among all study types, indicating that their results should be approached with caution.
references.relevance: Supports the broader argument that real-world/retrospective studies (the category EPI-PHARE/Epifar belongs to) are methodologically weak and systematically conclude there is no problem; the specific EPI-PHARE/Epifar study itself is not named in the book.

findings.summary: The book directly quotes and footnotes the McKinsey article advocating cutting vaccine development from sequence to authorization to 100 days, matching the todo precisely.

references.quote: The bar has risen, and there is now serious discussion of what it will take to cut the time from sequence to authorization to just 100 days for the next emerging threat.”
references.relevance: This is the verbatim McKinsey quotation advocating 100-day vaccine development that the todo seeks; it appears with footnote 257 linking to the McKinsey 'ten-lessons-from-the-first-two-years-of-COVID19' article.

references.quote: 100 days? When you see the “junk food” that we have on the market in 326 days, I let you imagine what awaits us in 100 days!
references.relevance: Cotton's commentary explicitly reacting to the McKinsey 100-day accelerated-development proposal, confirming this is the source she references and criticizes.

references.quote: Welcome to the new world, Reader friends, a new world in which the word precaution is now obsolete, a world in which consulting firms take care of your health
references.relevance: Frames the McKinsey passage as evidence of the accelerated-development model being normalized by consulting firms — the exact concern the todo raises (McKinsey-EU-pharma triangle / normalization of accelerated development).

findings.summary: The book devotes an entire section to the Maddie de Garay omission from the Pfizer 12-15 adolescent clinical study report, page-citing the SAE section and the FDA document, directly supporting this OPECST-linked follow-up.

references.quote: This is what is documented in the "SAE, Serious Adverse Events" section of the report detailing the excruciating suffering of this young girl. “One participant experienced a serious adverse event reported as generalized neuralgia, and also reported 3 concomitant non-serious events (abdominal pain, abscess, gastritis) and 1 concomitant adverse event (constipation) in the same week. The participant was eventually diagnosed with functional abdominal pain.”
references.relevance: Documents exactly how Maddie de Garay's disabling injury was recoded/omitted in the 12-15 adolescent clinical study report as mere 'functional abdominal pain' — the page-citable omission the todo asks to cross-link.

references.quote: Indeed, the vaccine safety evaluation only included 1,131 participants. With such a limited number, it was, of course, impossible to detect myocarditis during the trial. If Maddie's case had been reported, it would have highlighted a disability in 1,131 adolescents.
references.relevance: Quantifies the impact of the omission within the 12-15 trial (1,131 participants), the core of the Maddie de Garay omission argument referenced in the interview.

references.quote: Would BNT162b2 have been authorized for 12-15-year-olds if Maddie’s testimonies and complaints filed by Stephanie de Garay and Augusto Roux.
references.relevance: Ties the Maddie de Garay omission directly to the 12-15 adolescent authorization, the link the todo wants to page-cite alongside the OPECST hearing material.

findings.summary: The book directly and precisely addresses this: it pins Bourla's admission to the Washington Post on March 10, 2022, quoting him verbatim that mRNA 'never delivered a single product until that day.'

She co-founded a collective called React19.

findings.summary: Directly addressed: the book reproduces the PCR-confirmed case counts (8 vaccine vs 162 placebo) and presents Cotton's recomputed efficacy of ~53% using the anti-nucleocapsid serology criterion and the court-released SAS database.

references.quote: efficacy calculated based on anti-nucleocapsid serology, also known as non-S-seroconversion to SARS-CoV-2 infection, which includes all COVID cases, is approximately 53%, calculated using the SAS® database.
references.relevance: Confirms the analysis was run on the SAS database (the PHMPT/court-released datasets the todo asks about) yielding ~53% rather than 95%.

findings.summary: Directly addressed: the book documents the OPECST hearing (April 5, 2022), the OPECST interim report of June 9, 2022 with its rapporteurs and findings, and explicitly notes the transparency gap that Cotton's testimony was omitted from the published report.

findings.summary: Not addressed: the book discusses EU procurement opacity (von der Leyen's text messages with Bourla and the refusal to publish Pfizer contracts) but never mentions Valneva, its contract suspension, Commissioner Kyriakides, or the 4 billion-dose stockpile.

The book directly supports Cotton's case-detection-bias argument: it confirms in the database that vaccine-arm participants were tested less often than placebo participants, ties this and antipyretic use to undercounted COVID cases, and recalculates efficacy at ~53% on the serological criterion (vs. the 95% PCR endpoint).

references.quote: Moreover, we have confirmation in the database that participants who received the vaccine were tested less frequently than those in the placebo group, which introduced a major bias in counting the number of COVID-19 cases, the primary efficacy criterion, in favor of the vaccine150.
references.relevance: This is the book's explicit version of the interview claim that the vaccine arm had fewer tests; footnote 150 cites the OpenVAET C4591001 trial audit (openvaet.substack.com) drawn from the released data, the closest the book comes to naming the underlying SAS-table comparison.

references.quote: Upon recalculating based on the serological criterion using the data made publicly available by Mr. Siri, I’ve determined that the efficacy of the vaccine on ALL COVID cases, and not solely on mild or moderate symptomatic cases confirmed by PCR tests, is approximately 53%!
references.relevance: Matches the pull-quote's anti-N serology recalculation (interview said ~55%; book says ~53%) showing efficacy collapses from 95% when all COVID cases are counted, and credits the court-released data ('Mr. Siri').

references.quote: Well, it is a big deal when you know that in the results, the vaccinated people used much more antipyretics than the saltwater vaccinated people did. This constitutes what we call a statistical bias, i.e., an element likely to make the results deviate from their true value.
references.relevance: Supports the antipyretic limb of the case-detection-bias argument from the pull quote — vaccine-arm participants took fever-reducers more often, masking COVID symptoms and undercounting cases.

findings.summary: The book provides the litigation provenance Cotton works from: it explains the FDA was forced by a court decision (action led by Aaron Siri) to release the clinical-trial documents, notes the FDA's request to keep them secret for 75 years, points readers to phmpt.org, and lists the SAS-format database she asks be released — though it does not name PHMPT v. references.quote: The Ventavia case pertains to only three sites, encompassing a little over 1000 participants, yet this raises concerns about the management of all other sites.

findings.summary: Not meaningfully addressed: the book describes Cotton's January 2022 request for a full external audit of the Pfizer trial, her April 2022 OPECST testimony, and that ANSM's database is not freely accessible, but it does not document any official ANSM response to a joint Cotton-Verity submission of the January 2022 expertise report; Cotton notes only that the answers she received were 'confidential.'

The book does not name 'Jikkyleaks' or the '4444 patient IDs' specifically, but it directly and substantially addresses the same underlying data-integrity topic: the court-released SAS/XPT datasets, their CDISC non-compliance, and the anomalous recruitment ramp at the Argentine Cosme Argerich Central Military Hospital center.

references.quote: The XPT files, or "transport" files, are generated by the SAS® software used to program the analyses of the Pfizer/BioNtech clinical trial and originate from the laboratory itself. Any SAS® user can import them to convert them back into SAS® tables and check the results included in the clinical reports. The format of these tables, which constitute the database, is governed by pharmaceutical industry standards. They must comply with the structure established by CDISC (Clinical Data Interchange Standards Consortium). It is worth noting that the SAS® tables provided by Pfizer do not follow these recommendations; there are missing variables in the datasets, seemingly intended to complicate the calculations for those competent enough to reprogram them.
references.relevance: Names the court-released SAS XPT datasets and the CDISC standards the follow-up asks about, and documents that Pfizer's SAS tables omitted variables — the reproducibility/data-integrity concern Jikky's thread also pursued.

references.quote: The Argentinean site that recruited Augusto Roux included more than 5700 participants between April 2020 and November 2020, and this out of a total of 40,000. 10,000 participants were recruited across only 5 centers.
references.relevance: Quantifies the Argentine military-hospital recruitment ramp (the 'recruitment ramp' the follow-up flags), showing the center's outsized enrollment versus the whole trial.

references.quote: Thanks to documents made public by a court decision, several whistleblowers have been examining the files containing data reported by each participant's site for over a year now.

references.relevance: Directly the French 'poisoning' complaint Cotton references in the interview, with date (May 2022), jurisdiction (France), complainants ('several victims'), and target ('unknown parties').

findings.summary: The book discusses the trial's own follow-up schedule (visits at 1/6/12/24 months), criticizes the 1-to-6-month 'black hole' gap, and cites a WHO technical briefing on endpoints, but it does not cite any pre-2020 WHO guideline prescribing 6 months of follow-up for tolerance and 1 year for efficacy as the comparator the COVID trials departed from.
